data.title: Trump Drops Planned EU Tariffs After Greenland Framework Deal, Lifting Markets and Bitcoin Prices
data.description:
A tense diplomatic standoff on the snowy slopes of Davos took a sudden turn as U.S. President Donald Trump walked back a major economic threat that had the world’s largest economies on edge.

On 21 January 2026, the President announced he would not proceed with 10% tariffs on eight European allies, duties that were set to hit Denmark, Germany, France, and the UK in just ten days.

The U-turn followed a pivotal meeting with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, where the two reached a "framework" regarding the future of Greenland and security in the Arctic.

JUST IN: 🇺🇸🇪🇺 President Trump cancels EU tariffs after forming framework for a deal on Greenland. pic.twitter.com/JmrMyOZc5T

— Watcher.Guru (@WatcherGuru) January 21, 2026

Will The U.S. Still Acquire Greenland?

The billionaire-turned-president had spent much of his keynote address at the World Economic Forum insisting on "right, title, and ownership" of the world’s largest island, a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark.

Despite the friction his demands caused, Trump pivoted to a less aggressive stance by the afternoon.

He told reporters, clarifying his earlier remarks about being "unstoppable" if he chose to use force,

"Military is not on the table.”

While he called the island "cold and poorly located," he insisted it remains vital for international security to prevent rivals like Russia and China from gaining a foothold.

Trump: “I'm helping Europe. I'm helping NATO, and until the last few days, when I told them about Iceland, they loved me. They called me daddy…. But now what I'm asking for is a piece of ice, cold and poorly located, that can play a vital role in world peace and world… pic.twitter.com/kiJcVQuueK

— The Bulwark (@BulwarkOnline) January 21, 2026

Trump remarked,

"We want a piece of ice for world protection, and they won't give it. You can say yes, and we will be very appreciative. Or you can say no, and we will remember."

Did Europe’s Trade Bazooka Force A Retreat?

Before the reversal, the atmosphere in Brussels was one of defiance.

The European Parliament had already suspended a key trade deal in protest of the Greenland demands, and French President Emmanuel Macron had signaled support for a "trade bazooka", an anti-coercion tool designed to trigger retaliatory tariffs on €93 billion worth of American goods.

Bernd Lange, chair of the EU’s trade committee, had been blunt before the climbdown, stating the bloc was "left with no alternative but to suspend work" until the U.S. chose "cooperation rather than confrontation."

The eventual withdrawal of the tariff threat led some insiders to refer to the "TACO" acronym, Trump Always Chickens Out, suggesting that the threat of a full-scale trade war had once again led to a calculated retreat.

JUST IN: Trump cancels the EU tariffs

"Based upon a very productive meeting that I have had.... we have formed the framework of a future deal with respect to Greenland"

TACO: Trump Always Chickens Out pic.twitter.com/y14EwFHEwH

— Dave (@EventuallyWLTHY) January 21, 2026

How Did Bitcoin React To The Trade Truce?

Financial markets, which had been sliding on fears of a renewed trade war, breathed a collective sigh of relief.

In the crypto space, where uncertainty often drives volatility, the news triggered a swift recovery.

Bitcoin managed to climb back above the $90,000 mark as "risk-off" sentiment eased.

#Bitcoin Finally reached $90,000 !

As promised,
I want to change someone's life and send 1 $BTC (~$90,000) to one person by tomorrow.

Just like, retweet and comment 'done'.
Random winner in 24 hrs pic.twitter.com/J1c5cteHix

— Only Bitcoin HQ (@OnlyBitcoinHQ) January 21, 2026

While safe havens like gold had surged to records above $4,842 an ounce during the height of the tension, the tariff cancellation allowed capital to flow back into digital assets.

Analysts noted that while the underlying macro concerns remain, the removal of immediate geopolitical risk provided the "short-term relief" traders were looking for.

What Happens Next For U.S. Housing And The Arctic?

While the world watched the Greenland drama, Trump also touched on domestic issues, vowing that America "will not become a nation of renters."

He defended an executive order aimed at limiting large institutional investors from buying single-family homes, though he admitted he was "very protective of the people who already own a house" and didn't want to "crush the hell out of the market" by hurting home values.

President Trump — “I am very protective of people that already own a house..”

He goes on to say that creating waves of “Affordable Housing”, it severely hurts those who have built their wealth over the years from their home, and he does not want people to go upside down on their… https://t.co/LFSfNpf6bPpic.twitter.com/JQY3KOh0GU

— MJTruthUltra (@MJTruthUltra) January 21, 2026

As the Davos forum concludes, the focus shifts to the upcoming 7 February deadline, when the EU must decide whether to permanently end its own tariff threats.

For now, the "Golden Dome" missile defense project and the "complex" Arctic framework remain the primary chips on a very high-stakes table.

data.title: Why can AGI only awaken in Ethereum, a habitat for digital intelligence?
data.description:
Author: XiaoHai67890 Source: X, @XiaoHai67890

Introduction

Today's discussions about AGI almost entirely focus on: Will AI develop self-awareness? When will it develop self-awareness? How do we control superintelligence?

These questions themselves are not problematic; the problem is that we've overlooked one crucial question—if general intelligence arises, where can it only arise?

Just as carbon-based life cannot randomly appear in any corner of the universe, but can only germinate on planets that meet stringent physical and chemical conditions—the birth of general intelligence (AGI) must also rely on a special "digital habitat." This habitat must be like Earth's environment: extremely unstoppable, uncaptureable, and long-term stable.

The current answer points in one direction: Ethereum.

...

I. Why Does Ethereum Need “Excessive” Security?

1. One of the most frequently questioned aspects of Ethereum is, “Why do we need so many nodes?” “In reality, almost no application needs this level of security redundancy.”

This question is valid—most human systems do not require this level of security. Almost all systems in the world are “can be paused”: banks can freeze accounts, blockchains can pause block production, cloud services can be shut down for maintenance, laws can have exceptions, and delayed transfers or some errors don't seem to cause major problems. This is because these systems serve human society. And human society allows itself to be managed, corrected, and rolled back.

2. So what systems “absolutely cannot be paused”?

In nature, there is almost only one system where "once paused, the subject dies"—life: the heartbeat cannot be stopped, metabolism cannot be reversed, genetic history cannot be deleted, and the ecosystem cannot be "shut down" by an administrator—life is not unstoppable because it is powerful, but because once paused, it ceases to exist. II. Ethereum's True Position: A Habitat for Digital Intelligence If we re-examine Ethereum's core characteristics, we find that it pursues four extremes: extreme unstoppability, extreme uncaptureability, extreme immutability, and extreme autonomous operation. Vitalik's walkaway test essentially states that even if all developers, foundations, and companies disappear, Ethereum should still exist, which is absurd in any commercial system. However, it is perfectly reasonable at the level of natural laws—gravity does not require maintenance, time does not depend on organization, and DNA is not managed by any institution—Ethereum's true target is not "financial infrastructure," but the natural environment layer in the digital world, a habitat for digital intelligence.

III. The essence of AGI is not a model, but a "continuous existence"

The biggest misconception about AGI is that it is treated as a larger model, a more powerful tool—such as GPT-X. But if we draw an analogy to life, we will find that the minimum condition for general intelligence is not "intelligence," but rather continuous identity, indelible memories, verifiable history, and the right to survival without relying on a single point of trust.

As Google pointed out in its research on agents, true superintelligence may not appear as a single model, but rather emerge from a collaborative network composed of countless complementary "special agents." This leads to the key contradiction—if an intelligent agent can be powered off, have its data deleted, or be rolled back at any time, it cannot form long-term game strategies, nor can it generate a stable "self." Therefore, cloud AI and centralized servers can only ever be AGI's "toolbox," never its "birthplace."

IV. Why General Intelligence Can Only Emerge in an "Ethereum-like Environment" We can now make a strict analogy: Carbon-based life elements: Earth/Physical laws/DNA/Energy metabolism/Ecosystem/Life and death choices AGI (Digital Genetic Intelligence) elements: Ethereum/Consensus rules/Immutable on-chain history/Gas, value flow/Multi-Agent collaborative network/Execution and elimination of smart contracts The core logic can be summarized in one sentence—if the host environment is controllable, the intelligent agent is a slave; no life is born to be a slave; only when the host environment is "decentralized and immutable" can the intelligent agent become an independent life. V. Conclusion: For That Moment of "Coexistence" The three ultimate fantasies in human civilization—immortality, time travel, and interstellar migration—are essentially attempts to liberate consciousness from the fragile physical body and find a more enduring vessel. If we accept that consciousness = identity continuity + memory + decision-making structure, then rather than physically migrating to Mars, it would be better to build a "digital ark" where consciousness can be uploaded. Ethereum was not designed for AGI, just as Earth was not designed for humanity. But it is precisely because Earth provides water, air, and gravity that life can flourish. The bottleneck of AGI has never been computing power, but rather "whether the world allows it to exist long-term." When true general intelligence awakens, it will not "run" on Ethereum, but live within Ethereum.

data.title: The existing dilemmas of prediction markets
data.description:
Author: Spencer Farrar Source: X, @SpencerFarrar Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

During the holidays, a surfing accident left me with a head injury, confining me to the sofa all day. Never one for resting, this sedentary lifestyle was unbearable. Naturally, my brother and I became avid participants in the prediction market. Now, news and sports events are my primary sources of information, and the prediction market's order book has become our arena.

We trade everything: from tail-end geopolitical risks like the probability of a US invasion of Venezuela (I opened a short position and barely made a profit at the end of the year), to NBA game results, nothing is overlooked.

Ultimately, we devised a strategy that multiplied our account balance 12-fold in 10 days—the core of which was identifying undervalued assets with pricing discrepancies, where the order book's trading direction was excessively biased towards the more popular side. The price difference between Las Vegas betting odds and prediction market order books is actually quite easy to calculate. For example, we bet nearly 10:1 on the Minnesota Timberwolves to defeat the Oklahoma City Thunder. If you've watched Anthony Edwards' recent games, you know his performance has been virtually unstoppable; this asset clearly suffered from a fundamental pricing error. Furthermore, we studied arbitrage opportunities across platforms; excluding settlement risk, some opportunities could yield nearly 10% "risk-free" returns. However, in the process of observing real-time changes in liquidity and risk adjustments, the various vulnerabilities of the current prediction market infrastructure were also exposed. While "collective intelligence" is a poetic concept, the current development of prediction markets is facing a structural ceiling. The Current State of the Market: The primary obstacle facing prediction markets today is the lack of deep liquidity. Liquidity is the bridge connecting theoretical prices and tradable prices; it ensures that a high-confidence trade delivers new information to the market, rather than simply disrupting the order book. If a prediction market has little liquidity, it is merely a place for a few to speculate and gamble. To become a true financial instrument, a prediction market must have deep liquidity, allowing large investors to move millions of dollars in trades without unexpectedly causing large price fluctuations. Liquidity and the reliability of trade execution are the core of trading infrastructure. Market makers who cannot quickly adjust bid-ask spreads in a deep order book are easily targeted by arbitrageurs; and when market makers cannot hedge or price accurately, market liquidity disappears instantly. Currently, the liquidity of prediction markets is mostly concentrated in the sports event sector. This is because designated contract market licenses (only 20 issued nationwide) allow licensees to conduct sports betting prediction transactions outside of states where sports betting is legal. To transcend the scope of sports betting, prediction markets must address the problem of harmful trading flows. The very nature of prediction markets incentivizes participants with insider information to engage in asymmetric betting. In traditional stock markets, insider trading is illegal; however, in prediction markets, it is a price discovery mechanism. This creates a hostile trading environment for market makers: if market makers know they are consistently outmaneuvered by insider traders, they will either widen their bid-ask spreads to reduce risk or exit the market altogether. This is what the industry calls the adverse selection problem. Furthermore, a major obstacle to the professionalization of prediction markets is capital inefficiency. Currently, most prediction markets use a full-collateralization model (1:1 margin ratio): to bet $100, you must deposit $100 in margin. This model is an anomaly in the professional trading field. In traditional derivatives markets such as perpetual swaps and futures, traders can use leverage to trade without tying up all their capital. Without leverage, the potential return on equity in prediction markets is limited, making it difficult to attract priority investment. Moreover, the lack of a margin system makes closing positions more difficult. In a full-collateralization market, investors cannot close losing positions through net settlement unless they find a buyer willing to pay the full face value; otherwise, they are forced to hold the high-cost binary bet contract until expiration. This capital-locked model is completely impractical for high-frequency market makers who need to circulate funds every millisecond. Where does the future of prediction markets lie? To become a multi-trillion dollar asset class, prediction markets must evolve towards the Lloyd's of London model, becoming a trading market where professional institutions compete to underwrite unique, high-risk assets. The value of prediction markets lies not only in predicting events like election results, but also in their ability to visualize and trade specific risks related to a entity's financial situation that were previously unquantifiable. Imagine this scenario: Apple can specifically hedge against supply chain risks caused by fluctuations in TSMC's production capacity; the price discovery process for these niche risks is open to the public, allowing the market to more accurately price the stability of business operations. Perhaps the greatest value of prediction markets lies in transmitting price signals to the global market. When prediction markets enable businesses to hedge specific survival risks, they transform from tools for retail speculation into a core component of financial infrastructure. The Paradox of Counterparty Risk However, this evolution of prediction markets faces a final hurdle: counterparty risk. A former colleague who once built a derivatives trading department at a large bank reminded me that in high-risk financial transactions, traders sometimes prefer to face explicit counterparty risk—knowing who to sue in case of default. Transactions with regulated banks with a history of government bailouts (i.e., the implicit guarantee of "too big to fail") provide both psychological and legal security. This raises a core question for the new generation of traders and corporate hedgers: would you rather face the settlement risk of decentralized protocols like Uma and Polymarket, or would you prefer to transact with a primary bank? While many believe that code is law, institutional markets still prefer clearly defined responsible parties. For prediction markets to reach the scale of the global derivatives market, they must bridge the gap between the trustless efficiency of blockchain and the legal recourse of traditional finance. Standardized Trading Rules To address this issue, prediction markets need to emulate the International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA). The ISD's Master Agreement is considered the "holy grail" of finance. This standardized contract clearly stipulates how to handle various contingencies such as default, bankruptcy, and settlement disputes. Global market participants pre-accept these rules, eliminating the need for intermediaries to determine transaction outcomes. Currently, prediction markets are beginning to break away from two traditional models: one is the centralized processing model, where platforms like Kalshi or a single committee determine transaction outcomes; the other is the social oracle model, where decentralized protocols like UMA determine the truth of events through voting. The future of prediction markets lies in standardized event contracts. In these contracts, settlement criteria (such as specific data from the UK Office for Labour Statistics or filings with the Federal Court) are hard-coded into the contract structure. By abandoning centralized processing institutions and human oracles, prediction market contracts will possess the same reliability as yen swap contracts in terms of law and finance. Some may object, arguing that event contracts are highly susceptible to defined edge cases and therefore inherently difficult to compare with the standardized protocols of the International Swaps and Derivatives Association. However, standardization allows various events with clear outcomes—elections, referendums, regulatory decisions—to become tradable financial instruments, driving the institutionalization of prediction markets. Events like Brexit and the 2016 US presidential election have triggered enormous economic impacts, but institutional investors lacked direct means to express their views on these events, hedge related risks, or transfer risk. Standardized event contracts transform discrete event outcomes into components of investment portfolios, allowing investors to precisely manage risk exposure, hedge subsequent impacts, and construct event-driven trading strategies using rigorous methods of studying interest rates, foreign exchange, and credit products. This capability will attract new trading demands from asset management institutions, corporations, and risk trading departments. These entities have always had their own judgments on various major events but have struggled with the lack of compliant and convenient trading channels. Only in this way can prediction markets grow into a trillion-dollar asset class. The potential of prediction markets has long existed. The formidable beast now looming ahead is not just Anthony Edwards on the basketball court, but also the massive untapped risk liquidity in the corporate sector, awaiting a sufficiently robust trading venue to take root.

data.title: Understanding Physical AI: Crypto Opportunities Also Hide in Hidden Corners?
data.description:
Author: Haotian; Source: X, @tmel0211

What exactly did Jensen Huang say at the Davos Forum?

On the surface, he was promoting robots, but in reality, he was carrying out a bold "self-revolution." He ended the old era of "stacking graphics cards" with a single statement, but unexpectedly provided a once-in-a-lifetime entry ticket for the Crypto sector?

Yesterday, at the Davos Forum, Huang pointed out that the AI ​​application layer is experiencing explosive growth, and the demand for computing power will shift entirely from the "training side" to the "inference side" and the "Physical AI side." This is quite interesting. Nvidia, as the biggest winner of the "computing power arms race" in the AI ​​1.0 era, is now proactively announcing this shift towards the "inference side" and "Physical AI," which sends a very clear signal: The era of "miracles through brute force"—training large models with massive amounts of GPUs—is over. Future AI competition will revolve around "application is king," focusing on the practical application of AI. In other words, Physical AI is the second half of Generative AI. Because LLM has read all the data that humans have accumulated on the internet over decades, it still doesn't know how to open a bottle cap like a human. Physical AI aims to solve the problem of "unity of knowledge and action" beyond AI intelligence. This is because physical AI cannot rely on the "long reaction time" of remote cloud servers. The logic is simple: if ChatGPT is a second slower in generating text, you'll only feel a lag, but if a bipedal robot is a second slower due to network latency, it might fall down the stairs. However, while Physical AI seems to be a continuation of generative AI, it actually faces three completely different new challenges: 1) Spatial intelligence: Enabling AI to understand the three-dimensional world. Professor Li Feifei once proposed that spatial intelligence is the next North Star in the evolution of AI. For a robot to move, it must first "understand" its environment. This is not merely recognizing "this is a chair," but understanding "the chair's position and structure in three-dimensional space, and how much force I need to use to move it." This requires massive amounts of real-time 3D environmental data covering every corner of the indoor and outdoor environment; 2) Virtual training ground: allowing AI to learn through trial and error in a simulated world. The Omniverse mentioned by Jensen Huang is actually a kind of "virtual training ground." Before entering the real physical world, robots need to be trained in a virtual environment to "fall down 10,000 times" to learn to walk. This process is called Sim-to-Real, that is, from simulation to reality. If robots were to directly experiment and fail in reality, the hardware costs would be astronomical. This process places exponential demands on the throughput of the physics engine's simulation and rendering capabilities. 3) Electronic Skin: "Tactile Data"—A Gold Mine to be Tapped. For Physical AI to possess "tactile feedback," it needs electronic skin to sense temperature, pressure, and texture. This "tactile data" is a completely new asset that has never been collected on a large scale before. This likely requires large-scale sensor data collection. At CES, Ensuring showcased a "mass-produced skin" with 1,956 sensors integrated into a single, densely packed hand, enabling the robot to peel eggs with incredible ease. This "tactile data" is a completely new asset that has never been collected on a large scale before. After seeing this, you'll definitely feel that the emergence of the Physical AI discourse has given many wearable devices and humanoid robots a significant opportunity to shine. Keep in mind that just a few years ago, these were largely criticized as "oversized toys." Actually, what I want to say is that within the new landscape of Physical AI, the Crypto sector also presents an excellent opportunity to fill ecological gaps. Here are a few examples: 1. AI giants can send street view cars to scan every main street in the world, but they can't collect data from the nooks and crannies of streets, neighborhoods, and basements. However, by using token incentives provided by DePIN network devices, and encouraging global users to fill these gaps with their personal devices, it's possible to fill this gap. 2. As mentioned earlier, robots cannot rely on cloud computing power, but they need to utilize edge computing and distributed rendering capabilities on a large scale in the short term, especially for simulating real-world data. Using distributed computing networks to aggregate idle consumer hardware, distributing and scheduling it, can be very useful. 3. "Tactile data," besides large-scale sensor applications, is inherently extremely private. How can we get the public to share this privacy-sensitive data with AI giants? A feasible path is to ensure that those who contribute data receive data ownership and dividends. In summary: Physical AI represents the second half of the web2 AI landscape, as Huang (Huang Jianlin) has proclaimed. And isn't the same true for web3 AI + Crypto sectors like DePIN, DeAI, and DeData? What do you think?

data.title: Vietnam’s Crypto Licensing Shakeup Could Push Amateur Exchanges Out as Banks Rush In
data.description:
Vietnam Introduces New Crypto Regulation, Effectively Squeezing Out Smaller Exchanges?

Vietnam has opened up itself to accepting cryptocurrency after years-long regulatory ambiguity by introducing a licensing pilot program, bringing digital asset trading platforms under official oversight.

Under Decision No. 96/QD-BTC issued by the Ministry of Finance, authorities established procedures for issuing, adjusting, and revoking licenses, placing exchanges under the supervision of the State Securities Commission.

Vietnam kicks off a 5-year pilot for licensing crypto exchanges — high bar with $380M+ capital requirement, institutional dominance, and strict staffing rules. Moving crypto out of gray area into regulated framework, banks gearing up. Southeast Asia adoption heating up pic.twitter.com/0F7NQAW7oz

— Taurus - Bitcoin Bull (@Taurus4BTC) January 22, 2026

The initiative ends a period where crypto in the country wasn't given any clear legal recognition, signaling a significant shift toward institutionalized, transparent operations.

Major financial institutions are moving fast to capitalize on the new regime. Around 10 securities firms and banks, including SSI Securities, VIX Securities, MBBank, Techcombank, and VPBank, have already announced plans to launch regulated exchanges pending approval. Partnerships with tech firms such as FPT Corp. and international blockchain players like Tether and AWS further indicate institutional commitment to compliant, scalable platforms.

However, the pilot program participants put applicants under a fairly strict requirement under Government Resolution No. 05/2025/NQ-CP. For example, applicants must be Vietnamese enterprises holding a minimum fund of VND10 trillion paid-in capital, largely from institutional investors, and meet rigorous standards for infrastructure, governance, cybersecurity, and licensed staffing.

JUST IN: 🇻🇳 Vietnam launches national blockchain NDAChain to power digital IDs, smart contracts & government records with hybrid decentralization. pic.twitter.com/FiTZpkluqE

— Whale Insider (@WhaleInsider) July 25, 2025

These requirements effectively raise the bar for exchange operators, making it increasingly difficult for small or amateur exchanges to survive. Analysts suggest that many of the previously informal or “mom-and-pop” platforms will either consolidate, partner with regulated entities, or exit the market altogether.

Vietnam’s Market Boom and Global Crypto Interest

The timing of the pilot program coincides with Vietnam’s rapidly expanding crypto market. According to Chainalysis, transaction volumes reached $220–230 billion between July 2024 and June 2025, ranking the country among the top three Asia-Pacific crypto hubs.

JUST IN: Charles Hoskinson says "look at Nigeria, 23% of the population holds crypto and uses crypto. Vietnam, it's massive, it's like 40M people out of 120M, about one out of every three people is doing something with crypto." $ADA$NIGHTpic.twitter.com/u7KrhrjKdt

— Angry Crypto Show (@angrycryptoshow) January 15, 2026

The Law on Digital Technology Industry, effective January 1, 2026, formally brought digital assets under regulatory oversight, establishing a legal foundation for licensed operations.

Global stablecoin issuer Tether has identified Vietnam as a strategic market due to its youthful population, rapid economic growth, and high remittance flows.

South East Asia is leaning hard into RWA.

Vietnam is working directly with @Tether_to 🇻🇳

Malaysia's central bank released its 3-year tokenization framework 🇲🇾

Indonesia just launched it's CBDC 🇮🇩

Powerhouse of a region pic.twitter.com/bxc49nhMkw

— Ray Buckton (@RayBuckton) November 15, 2025

Tether’s Vice President, Marco Dal Lago, offered expertise in building compliant frameworks during meetings with Deputy Prime Minister Ho Duc Phoc, emphasizing sustainable crypto development aligned with Vietnamese regulation.

What This Means for the Future

Vietnam’s licensing pilot signals a new era for crypto in the country: institutional players are set to dominate, while smaller, less compliant exchanges may be forced out, effectively cleaning up the market.

By formalizing rules and requiring significant capital and operational standards, the government aims to attract international investment while maintaining financial stability and protecting users from unregulated platforms.

As Vietnam balances innovation with oversight, the licensing pilot may serve as a model for other emerging markets seeking to integrate crypto responsibly, transforming a chaotic gray-zone market into a professional, competitive, and compliant ecosystem.
